entry.title: A Look Back on my SAP Sapphire 2026 Experience
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando is officially in the books, and I am still riding the high from one of the most energizing weeks of the year. Between presenting the SAP HANA Cloud overview session and working the HANA Cloud booth, I had the pleasure to connect with hundreds of data professionals — answering questions, sparking conversations, and sharing potential database solutions. Here is a quick recap of what went down for me.

The Session: AI Starts with the Right Database

This overview presentation tackled two critical challenges that data teams are facing right now. First, the importance of bringing together every type and size of dataset in an organization — structured, unstructured, relational, graph, vector, spatial — in a way that actually makes AI smarter. Second, equipping data professionals with AI-driven tools so they can ship solutions faster without sacrificing quality.

As a single, cloud-native, in-memory database engine, SAP HANA Cloud supports analytics, applications, and AI from one platform. Its multi-model architecture means a singular database instance can simultaneously serve a transactional sales process, offer product recommendations using a vector engine, guide the customer experience using a knowledge graph, and increase operational efficiency thanks to spatial awareness. Additionally, the newly released SAP HANA Cloud agents reduce the SQL complexity and due diligence time that typically slows development teams down, enabling natural language access to database objects and relationships through an autogenerated knowledge graph.

The SAP HANA Cloud Booth: Four Questions That Kept Coming Back

Working the HANA Cloud booth proved just as rewarding as the session. Four questions surfaced repeatedly, and they are worth sharing here.

  • What is SAP Business Data Cloud? SAP Business Data Cloud is a suite of data management solutions — including SAP Datasphere, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Warehouse, and SAP Databricks — that work together to enable a business data fabric. Customers purchase BDC capacity units and apply them across these solutions based on their needs.
  • What does it mean that SAP HANA Cloud is now in SAP Business Data Cloud? HANA Cloud is an in-memory, multi-tiered, multi-model database capable of powering transactional applications, analytics, data science workflows, and AI use cases. Its inclusion in Business Data Cloud means customers now have access to a complete, unified set of data solutions.
  • How is SAP S/4HANA different from SAP HANA Cloud? S/4HANA is an ERP cloud solution built on a version of the HANA database. HANA Cloud is a standalone database that customers provision to power their own custom applications, analytics, or AI solutions.
  • Do I need SAP Business Data Cloud for AI? Absolutely. The effectiveness of any AI solution is directly tied to the quality, quantity, and quickness of the data behind it. SAP Business Data Cloud weaves together all data — SAP and non-SAP — into a singular business data fabric that is ready for Joule and other AI solutions. From a bigger picture perspective, the three domains of applications, data, and AI are intertwining so tightly that any one cannot exist effectively without the other two.

Keep the Momentum Going

If SAP Sapphire sparked your curiosity, do not let it stop there. Explore the free SAP HANA Cloud basic trial or the free SAP Business Data Cloud trial to get hands-on experience with these data solutions.

Have a topic about SAP HANA Cloud that is still fuzzy? Join the Data Professionals Community to learn, share, and grow with other data enthusiasts. Then, drop that question in the comments or create a discussion post — the conversation is just getting started.

entry.title: Re: Server didn't start after reboot
entry.content:
A very similar thing happened to me while installilng 16.1, pretty much the same messages

 

<<The context allocation routine failed when it tried to load localization files!!
One or more following problems may caused the failure

Your sybase home directory is /opt/sybase. Check the environment variable SYBASE if it is not the one you want!
Using locale name "C.UTF-8" defined in environment variable LANG
Locale name "C.UTF-8" doesn't exist in your /opt/sybase/locales/locales.dat file>>>

 

 

In my case this was solved by making sure the environment variables were properly loaded before running the 'startserver -f ' line.

You can verify this by running 'env' after your 'source SYBASE.sh' to make sure all SYBASE and SAP variables are set before attempting to start the server.  We make sure of this by adding    '. /opt/sybase/SYBASE.sh'  to the .bash_profile of the user starting the server.  That way the environment is always ready for either the server or any utility (isql, bcp, ddlgen, etc.)

HTH

entry.title: Re: How can we display details of the KPI direct from the...
entry.content:
The key concept here: KPIs show the number, dashboards show the context. For manufacturing SMBs who need actionable inventory visibility, here's what I recommend setting up in SAP B1 v9.x:

Core inventory KPIs every Hyderabad manufacturer should track:

KPI What it tells you

Stock-Out Risk (On Hand vs. Reorder Point)Items about to run dry
Inventory Turnover RatioHow fast stock moves — low = dead capital
Cycle Count VarianceAccuracy of your physical counts vs. system
Warehouse Occupancy %Space utilization across bins
FG Batch AgeDays since last movement — old batches = quality risk

To get the detail view from a dashboard tile:

  1. Open your Advanced Dashboard in SAP B1.
  2. Click on the KPI tile → go to Actions → Link to Form.
  3. Select the underlying query's base form (e.g., Item Ledger for stock movements).
  4. This opens the drill-down report when you click the KPI tile in production.

For manufacturing firms with batch management (particularly pharma in Medchal/Patancheru), I'd also add: "Released vs. On-Hold FG Batch Count" — a custom KPI that flags how many FG batches are awaiting QC release. This prevents you from accidentally selling or consuming unreleased material.

Custom KPIs in SAP B1 are built via the KPI Navigator (Inventory → Reporting → KPI Navigator). Use OITM, OITW, and OIVL as your base tables.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Retirement of Sales Cloud (V1) Classroom Training
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

On behalf of the Product Learning Team, I wanted to share an important update regarding our Sales Cloud (V1) a.k.a C4C  Classroom Training .

We are announcing the retirement of the following  Sales Cloud (V1) Classroom Trainings, effective June 30th.

  • C4H410 - SAP Sales Cloud 
  • C4H440 - SAP Cloud for Customer Administration 

We will be honouring all existing bookings, however we will not be accepting any new bookings going forward.

What is Available to You?

Not to worry — all of the content is still fully accessible! Here is what is available to support your learning:

  • Learning Journey — All classroom content is available at your own pace and we are actively updating this content. Check out the full Learning Journey here.
  • Hands-On Practice System — A live practice environment is available for self-paced learning
  • Expert Live Sessions — We will be running periodic sessions on LSC to answer any questions you may have on this solution.

Please Note

This retirement is specific to Sales Cloud (V1) only.

The Sales Cloud (V2) Instructor-Led Training sessions are very much live and available to book here: https://training.sap.com/course/c4h470-sap-sales-cloud-version-2-classroom-052-us-en

You can also check out the full Sales Cloud (V2) Learning Journey here.

Thank you for your attention and happy learning!

Hi Thomas,

my customer would like the trucks to be balanced in terms of quantity of desinations they handle. My customer would like the trucks (considering that human beings are inside and are part of a group that observe what other trucks do) be as equitative in the distribution as they can. But this is what is really wanted.  Please, can you help me with a suggestion? Could the BAdIs serve?

entry.title: Re: Unable to confirm putaway task with change bin or Exc...
entry.content:
Dear @taradmn,

For changing the destination bin during putaway confirmation, use CHBD Change Destination Bin. SAP confirms that in Process Warehouse Tasks Putaway, when the destination bin is changed the system can automatically enter the relevant exception code. SAP also confirms that if exception codes must be used during manual WT confirmation the correct app is Confirm Warehouse Tasks Advanced, where storage bin, HU and quantities can be adjusted

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3069905

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3495050

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/fdf6ba4109584a96944fbdb849cbb41c.html

The error in the screenshot suggests that CHBD is configured or triggered as a delivery-adjustment exception, which is not correct for this case. CHBD should be used only for changing the destination bin. Delivery quantity adjustment should be handled by a separate quantity-difference exception, not by CHBD

Recommended action:

  1. Use Process Warehouse Tasks Putaway for a standard destination-bin change or Confirm Warehouse Tasks Advanced when manual exception handling, quantity adjustment or split processing is required
  2. In SSCUI 104743 Define Exception Codes, check that CHBD is assigned to the correct putaway confirmation step and internal process for Change Destination Bin
  3. Remove/avoid delivery-adjustment configuration for CHBD
  4. For foreground split use the split/quantity adjustment functions in Confirm Warehouse Tasks Advanced and maintain the relevant exception code if required
  5. The second error indicates that Dest. Stor. Section is hidden or not available in the current Fiori layout, so the field visibility/layout and bin-related determination should be checked

Summary:

CHBD is the right exception for changing the destination bin but it should not be configured as a delivery-adjustment exception. For foreground split and manual exception handling use Confirm Warehouse Tasks Advanced

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Update settlement rule of maintenance order
entry.content:
Our requirement is to automatically default the settlement rule depending on the technical object type of the equipment or functional location. The receiver will be based on a custom table, with fields such as the technical object type, settlement category, receiver, and order type at least. The current configuration is that the settlement rules are generated during release. Since there are a couple of ways to release an order (via GUI tcodes, WDA apps, UI5 apps), the problem is which exit/badi/bapi to use. I have read that the exit EXIT_SAPLCOI1_027 is only applicable for GUI transactions and this is what our developer also mentioned. How else can we cater to the initial requirement where all possible entry points are covered?

Any input is greatly appreciated!

Regards,

Joanna

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting: Handling Standard and Te...
entry.content:
Hi Florian,

Thank you very much — this is extremely helpful and clarifies several things for us. You are right that we are doing translation into the group presentation currency, not the IAS 21 remeasurement step (our source ERP handles that upstream). Our use of "Temporal" was specifically referring to the SAP GR translation variant for subsidiaries classified as integrated foreign operations under IAS 21, where translation differences post to P&L rather than to OCI. We will use clearer terminology going forward.

A few quick follow-ups if I may:

  1. To confirm our design direction — we plan to use the Currency Translation Selection attribute on the FS Item as the driver, so the same FS Item (for example, Land) carries one nature-based selection. The translation method assigned to the consolidation unit then determines the actual rate applied (closing rate for self-sustaining units, historical rate for integrated units). Does this approach align with delivered content design?
  2. Thank you for the pointer on the FS Item Role for the OCI Translation Reserve. Is there a specific delivered Role value we should use, or should we look this up in our system?
  3. Could you point us to the specific delivered translation methods you would recommend as a starting point for IFRS reporting in S/4HANA GR on-premises 2023? We would like to adhere to delivered content as much as possible before considering any customization.
  4. One forward-looking note — our go-live is 2027, which is after IFRS 18 becomes effective (January 2027). Is there any SAP GR delivered content or guidance for handling translation differences across the new IFRS 18 P&L categories (Operating, Financing, etc.)?

Thanks again — this has been very clarifying.

Best regards,

NavT

entry.title: How to update Username in SAP SuccessFactors - 1H 2026 update FAIL
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

After a very “character-building” 1H 2026 SuccessFactors update weekend — complete with a Latest People Profile RBP adventure related to Employee Views — we are continuing to discover additional surprises that somehow did not make themselves obvious in the “What’s New Viewer.” Think less “hidden feature” and more “new puppy surprise found in the dark with bare feet in the kitchen.”

Today’s episode is brought to you by KBA 2457516 — How to update Username in SAP SuccessFactors.

Prior to the upgrade, system administrators could directly update Basic/General Info within an associate’s profile. This was particularly useful in the occasional scenario where a newly onboarded associate received a generated username that conflicted with an inactive associate still sitting in SuccessFactors, despite already being purged from Azure.

The old process was beautifully simple:

  1. Attempt to assign the username to the new associate.
  2. Receive the very helpful message that the username already existed.
  3. Locate the inactive profile.
  4. Add an “X” to the end of the old username.
  5. Assign the intended username to the new associate.

Total elapsed time: about two minutes.
Stress level: peachy.
Application errors: mysteriously nonexistent.

Then came the 1H 2026 release, which politely informed us: “Absolutely not.”

Apparently, direct updates are now restricted due to the risk of “inconsistent behavior or application errors.” While I appreciate SAP’s concern for our wellbeing, I should note we had been performing this exact process for years without unleashing any known chaos into the system.

The new process now resembles an HRIT escape room challenge:

  • Download the UDF via Associate Export
  • Locate the active associate record
  • Build a Basic Import file
  • Navigate to Import Associate Data
  • Validate the file
  • Run the import
  • Wait for successful completion
  • Discover the username still did not update
  • Wonder briefly if reality itself is broken

After additional research, we then discover there is still an inactive associate using the generated username. So now:

  • Return to the UDF
  • Modify the inactive username with an “X” suffix
  • Rebuild the import file
  • Re-import using the “Process Inactive Associates” checkbox that absolutely cannot be forgotten
  • Wait for Scheduled Job Manager to bless the transaction
  • Rebuild and reimport the file again for the new associate
  • Refresh the profile repeatedly like a day trader watching stock prices

Finally… success.

Total elapsed time: approximately 15–20 minutes.
Application errors prevented: still apparently zero.
Administrative morale: under active investigation.

Apologies for the sarcasm, but after spending most of the weekend troubleshooting profile and permission issues tied to provisioning jobs and RBP migration steps that behaved differently in Production than Preview, my sense of humor is now functioning as a coping mechanism.

The good news is we are continuing to work through the issues.
The bad news is I now instinctively distrust any sentence beginning with “This enhancement improves the user experience.”

HAGD,

Garrett Meredith
Disgruntled System Admin, Esq.

entry.title: Deploying SAP HANA 2.0 on AWS Using Terraform and Ansible
entry.content:
Introduction

This post is a trimmed-down version of the core automation work from my MSc final project, adapted here as a practical walkthrough for the SAP Community. I've left out the React UI side of things — Terraform and Ansible are dyed-in-the-wool command line tools, and covering the UI layer in full would make this a very different (and much longer) post.

The goal was a fully automated SAP HANA 2.0 SPS08 deployment on AWS, driven from a React web UI. Terraform provisions the infrastructure, Ansible handles the HANA installation, and a Node.js backend streams the output live to the browser. 

Prerequisites

You'll need the following installed and configured locally before you start:

  • Terraform (v1.0+) — terraform.io
  • Ansible (v2.12+) — pip install ansible
  • AWS CLI v2 — aws.amazon.com/cli
  • AWS credentials configured with sufficient IAM permissions to create EC2 instances, IAM roles, instance profiles, and security groups (aws configure or a named profile)

Cost: An r5.xlarge in us-east-2 runs at roughly $0.25/hour. The SUSE SLES for SAP PAYG AMI includes the SUSE subscription in that cost — no separate licence to worry about. A full deployment and verification run takes under an hour, so a single test should cost you around $0.25–$0.50. Just remember to terminate the instance when you're done.

Architecture

React UI (browser)
    │
    ▼
Node.js / Express (server.js)
    ├── Terraform (provision EC2)
    └── Ansible (install HANA via SSH)
              │
              ▼
         AWS EC2 (SUSE SLES for SAP 15 SP5 PAYG)
              │
              └── S3 (HANA media)

Infrastructure — Terraform

Why SUSE SLES for SAP PAYG?

HANA requires SUSE Linux Enterprise Server for SAP Applications — not standard SLES, and not CentOS/RHEL. The PAYG (pay-as-you-go) AMI is the right choice on AWS because it includes the SUSE subscription built into the instance cost. BYOS (bring your own subscription) requires a separate SUSE registration step that complicates automation.

For us-east-2 (Ohio):

locals {
  sles_sap_ami = "ami-099afc29551c4b139"  # SUSE SLES for SAP 15 SP5 PAYG, us-east-2
}

Minimum Viable Instance for HANA

SAP's official minimum for HANA is 32GB RAM. The smallest AWS instance that meets this is r5.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32GB). It's not certified for production but works fine for dev/test.

resource "aws_instance" "hana" {
  ami                    = local.sles_sap_ami
  instance_type          = "r5.xlarge"
  key_name               = var.key_pair_name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.hana.id]
  iam_instance_profile   = aws_iam_instance_profile.hana_s3.name

  root_block_device {
    volume_type = "gp3"
    volume_size = 100  # 50GB isn't enough — HANA media (15GB) + install fills it
  }

  tags = { Name = "hana-server" }
}

Gotcha: Start with at least 100GB. The HANA media alone is ~15GB and the installation needs room to expand. A 50GB volume will fail mid-install with a disk full error.

IAM — S3 Access for HANA Media

Rather than baking credentials into the instance, we attach an IAM instance profile that grants read access to the S3 bucket holding the HANA media:

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-read"
  role = aws_iam_role.hana_s3.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
      Resource = [
        "arn:aws:s3:::${var.hana_media_bucket}",
        "arn:aws:s3:::${var.hana_media_bucket}/*"
      ]
    }]
  })
}

HANA Media — S3

Upload the HANA media once. The Ansible playbook pulls it down on each deployment:

aws s3 sync ~/Downloads/51058674/ s3://your-hana-bucket/SAP_HANA_DATABASE/ \
  --region us-east-2 --no-progress --exclude "*.DS_Store"

The hdblcm installer lives at:

s3://your-hana-bucket/SAP_HANA_DATABASE/DATA_UNITS/HDB_SERVER_LINUX_X86_64/hdblcm

The Ansible Playbook

OS Prerequisites

HANA has specific OS requirements. Key tasks:

Swap:

- name: Create swapfile
  command: dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1M count={{ swap_size_mb }}
  when: swap_check.stdout == ""

Kernel parameters:

- name: Set kernel parameters for HANA
  sysctl:
    name: "{{ item.key }}"
    value: "{{ item.value }}"
    sysctl_set: true
    reload: true
  loop:
    - { key: "vm.max_map_count",  value: "2147483647" }
    - { key: "kernel.sem",        value: "1250 256000 100 8192" }
    - { key: "kernel.numa_balancing", value: "0" }

Transparent Huge Pages — must be disabled:

- name: Disable THP at runtime
  shell: |
    echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
    echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag

saptune (SUSE's SAP tuning tool):

- name: Apply SAP HANA tuning profile
  command: saptune solution apply HANA

Pulling HANA Media from S3

The instance profile handles authentication — no keys needed:

- name: Sync HANA media from S3
  command: >
    aws s3 sync s3://{{ hana_s3_bucket }}/{{ hana_s3_prefix }}/ {{ hana_media_dir }}/
    --no-progress

The Silent Install — hdblcm Response File

HANA's installer (hdblcm) supports unattended installation via a response file. We generate it with a Jinja2 template rather than Ansible's copy module — the copy module's YAML block scalar adds leading whitespace that hdblcm's parser chokes on.

hana_install.cfg.j2:

[Server]
action=install
components=server
sid={{ hana_sid }}
number={{ hana_instance }}
password={{ hana_master_pw }}
sapadm_password={{ sapadm_password }}
system_user_password={{ hana_master_pw }}
lss_user_password={{ lss_password }}
lss_backup_password={{ lss_password }}
system_usage=custom
install_execution_mode=optimized
sapmnt={{ hana_install_dir }}
datapath={{ hana_data_dir }}/{{ hana_sid }}
logpath={{ hana_log_dir }}/{{ hana_sid }}
ignore=check_min_mem
- name: Write hdblcm silent response file
  template:
    src: hana_install.cfg.j2
    dest: /tmp/hana_install.cfg
    mode: "0600"

Gotcha: The lss_user_password and lss_backup_password fields are required from HANA 2.0 SPS06 onwards. LSS (Log Segment Store) was optional in earlier releases but is now mandatory. If you omit these fields, hdblcm will fail silently with a cryptic parameter error.

Moving LSS Media Aside

The HANA media bundle includes an LSS component folder. If hdblcm detects it, it tries to install it and expects additional parameters. Moving it aside before running the installer avoids this:

- name: Move LSS media aside
  command: mv {{ hana_media_dir }}/lss {{ hana_media_dir }}/lss_aside
  args:
    removes: "{{ hana_media_dir }}/lss"

Running hdblcm — Async is Essential

The HANA installation takes 20–40 minutes. Without async, the SSH connection will time out and kill the install mid-way:

- name: Run SAP HANA installation (hdblcm)
  command: >
    {{ hdblcm_path }} --batch --configfile=/tmp/hana_install.cfg
  register: hdblcm_result
  changed_when: true
  async: 3600
  poll: 30

async: 3600 lets the task run for up to an hour. poll: 30 checks back every 30 seconds. Ansible keeps the job alive on the remote end even if the control connection drops.

Verify Installation

- name: Check HDB daemon is running
  shell: |
    su - {{ hana_sid | lower }}adm -c "HDB info" 2>&1 | grep -E "hdbdaemon|hdbnameserver"
  register: hdb_info
  failed_when: hdb_info.rc != 0

Streaming Output to the Browser

The Node.js backend uses child_process.spawn to run both Terraform and Ansible, streaming stdout/stderr as chunked HTML to an iframe. This means you can watch the install progress in real time without polling.

const proc = spawn('ansible-playbook', [
  '-i', `ec2-user@${ip},`,
  '--private-key', keyPath,
  '-e', extraVars,
  playbookPath
], { env: { ...process.env, ANSIBLE_FORCE_COLOR: '1' } });

proc.stdout.on('data', chunk => {
  res.write(`<pre>${chunk}</pre>`);
});

Key Lessons

Problem Fix

BYOS AMI requires SUSE registrationUse PAYG AMI instead
50GB disk fills during installUse 100GB minimum
SSH timeout kills hdblcmasync: 3600, poll: 30
hdblcm config with leading spacesUse template module, not copy
lss_user_password missingRequired from SPS06+ — include it
hdblcm finds LSS media and failsMove lss folder aside before install
macOS SSH key permission deniedMove PEM to ~/.ssh/, xattr -c, chmod 400

Result

A fully automated HANA 2.0 SPS08 installation on a fresh SUSE SLES for SAP instance in under 40 minutes, triggered from a browser. The same pattern extends to NetWeaver, S/4HANA, or any other SAP component that supports hdblcm or sapinst unattended install.

Appendix A — Full Ansible Playbook

server/ansible/hana-install.yml

---
- name: SAP HANA prerequisites and installation
  hosts: all
  become: true
  gather_facts: true

  vars:
    hana_sid: "HDB"
    hana_instance: "00"
    hana_master_pw: "<your-hana-master-password>"
    sapadm_password: "<your-sapadm-password>"
    lss_password: "<your-lss-password>"
    hana_s3_bucket: "<your-s3-bucket-name>"
    hana_s3_prefix: "SAP_HANA_DATABASE/DATA_UNITS/HDB_SERVER_LINUX_X86_64"
    hana_media_dir: "/hana/media"
    hana_install_dir: "/hana/shared"
    hana_data_dir: "/hana/data"
    hana_log_dir: "/hana/log"
    swap_size_mb: 20480

  tasks:

    - name: Confirm target is SUSE
      fail:
        msg: "This playbook requires SUSE Linux Enterprise Server. Got: {{ ansible_os_family }}"
      when: ansible_os_family != 'Suse'

    - name: Refresh zypper repositories
      zypper_repository:
        repo: '*'
        runrefresh: true
      ignore_errors: true

    - name: Install required OS packages
      zypper:
        name:
          - libaio1
          - libaio-devel
          - libltdl7
          - libatomic1
          - libnuma1
          - numactl
          - uuidd
          - rsync
          - lsof
          - unzip
          - xfsprogs
          - glibc-i18ndata
          - graphviz
          - expect
          - python3
        state: present

    - name: Enable and start uuidd
      systemd:
        name: uuidd
        state: started
        enabled: true

    - name: Check existing swap
      command: swapon --show=SIZE --noheadings --bytes
      register: swap_check
      changed_when: false
      failed_when: false

    - name: Create swapfile
      command: dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1M count={{ swap_size_mb }}
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Set swapfile permissions
      file:
        path: /swapfile
        mode: "0600"
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Format swapfile
      command: mkswap /swapfile
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Activate swapfile
      command: swapon /swapfile
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Persist swapfile in fstab
      lineinfile:
        path: /etc/fstab
        line: "/swapfile swap swap defaults 0 0"
        state: present

    - name: Set kernel parameters for HANA
      sysctl:
        name: "{{ item.key }}"
        value: "{{ item.value }}"
        sysctl_set: true
        state: present
        reload: true
      loop:
        - { key: "vm.max_map_count",            value: "2147483647" }
        - { key: "kernel.sem",                   value: "1250 256000 100 8192" }
        - { key: "net.core.somaxconn",           value: "4096" }
        - { key: "net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog", value: "8192" }
        - { key: "kernel.numa_balancing",        value: "0" }

    - name: Set ulimits for SAP
      copy:
        dest: /etc/security/limits.d/99-sap.conf
        mode: "0644"
        content: |
          @sapsys  soft  nofile  1048576
          @sapsys  hard  nofile  1048576
          @sapsys  soft  nproc   unlimited
          @sapsys  hard  nproc   unlimited
          @sdba    soft  nofile  1048576
          @sdba    hard  nofile  1048576

    - name: Disable THP at runtime
      shell: |
        echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
        echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag
      changed_when: true

    - name: Create systemd unit to disable THP on boot
      copy:
        dest: /etc/systemd/system/disable-thp.service
        mode: "0644"
        content: |
          [Unit]
          Description=Disable Transparent Huge Pages
          After=network.target
          [Service]
          Type=oneshot
          ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled && echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag"
          RemainAfterExit=yes
          [Install]
          WantedBy=multi-user.target

    - name: Enable disable-thp service
      systemd:
        name: disable-thp
        enabled: true
        daemon_reload: true

    - name: Create HANA filesystem directories
      file:
        path: "{{ item }}"
        state: directory
        mode: "0755"
      loop:
        - "{{ hana_install_dir }}"
        - "{{ hana_data_dir }}"
        - "{{ hana_log_dir }}"
        - "/hana/backup"

    - name: Install saptune
      zypper:
        name: saptune
        state: present
      ignore_errors: true

    - name: Apply SAP HANA tuning profile via saptune
      command: saptune solution apply HANA
      changed_when: true
      ignore_errors: true

    - name: Enable saptune daemon
      systemd:
        name: saptune
        enabled: true
        state: started
      ignore_errors: true

    - name: Check if aws cli is present
      command: which aws
      register: aws_cli_check
      changed_when: false
      failed_when: false

    - name: Download and install AWS CLI v2
      shell: |
        curl -fsSL "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o /tmp/awscliv2.zip
        unzip -q /tmp/awscliv2.zip -d /tmp/awscli-install
        /tmp/awscli-install/aws/install --bin-dir /usr/local/bin --install-dir /usr/local/aws-cli --update
        rm -rf /tmp/awscliv2.zip /tmp/awscli-install
      when: aws_cli_check.rc != 0

    - name: Create remote media staging directory
      file:
        path: "{{ hana_media_dir }}"
        state: directory
        mode: "0755"

    - name: Sync HANA media from S3
      command: >
        aws s3 sync s3://{{ hana_s3_bucket }}/{{ hana_s3_prefix }}/ {{ hana_media_dir }}/
        --no-progress
      register: s3_sync
      changed_when: "'download' in s3_sync.stdout"

    - name: Find hdblcm installer
      find:
        paths: "{{ hana_media_dir }}"
        patterns: "hdblcm"
        recurse: true
      register: hdblcm_find

    - name: Fail if hdblcm not found
      fail:
        msg: "hdblcm not found under {{ hana_media_dir }} — check hana_s3_bucket and hana_s3_prefix"
      when: hdblcm_find.files | length == 0

    - name: Set hdblcm path fact
      set_fact:
        hdblcm_path: "{{ hdblcm_find.files[0].path }}"

    - name: Make media directory fully executable
      shell: chmod -R 755 {{ hana_media_dir }}
      changed_when: true

    - name: Move LSS media aside
      command: mv {{ hana_media_dir }}/lss {{ hana_media_dir }}/lss_aside
      args:
        removes: "{{ hana_media_dir }}/lss"

    - name: Write hdblcm silent response file
      template:
        src: hana_install.cfg.j2
        dest: /tmp/hana_install.cfg
        mode: "0600"

    - name: Run SAP HANA installation (hdblcm)
      command: >
        {{ hdblcm_path }} --batch --configfile=/tmp/hana_install.cfg
      register: hdblcm_result
      changed_when: true
      async: 3600
      poll: 30

    - name: Show hdblcm output
      debug:
        msg: "{{ hdblcm_result.stdout_lines }}"

    - name: Check HDB daemon is running
      shell: |
        su - {{ hana_sid | lower }}adm -c "HDB info" 2>&1 | grep -E "hdbdaemon|hdbnameserver"
      register: hdb_info
      changed_when: false
      failed_when: hdb_info.rc != 0

    - name: Confirm HANA installation complete
      debug:
        msg: >
          SAP HANA {{ hana_sid }} instance {{ hana_instance }} installed successfully.
          Connect: hdbsql -i {{ hana_instance }} -u SYSTEM -p <password>

Appendix B — Terraform Configuration

main.tf

locals {
  sles_sap_ami = "ami-099afc29551c4b139"  # SUSE SLES for SAP 15 SP5 PAYG, us-east-2
}

resource "aws_iam_role" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect    = "Allow"
      Principal = { Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com" }
      Action    = "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-read"
  role = aws_iam_role.hana_s3.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
      Resource = [
        "arn:aws:s3:::${var.hana_media_bucket}",
        "arn:aws:s3:::${var.hana_media_bucket}/*"
      ]
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_instance_profile" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-profile"
  role = aws_iam_role.hana_s3.name
}

resource "aws_security_group" "hana" {
  name        = "hana-sg"
  description = "SSH access for HANA deployment"

  ingress {
    from_port   = 22
    to_port     = 22
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = { Name = "hana-sg" }
}

resource "aws_instance" "hana" {
  ami                    = local.sles_sap_ami
  instance_type          = "r5.xlarge"
  key_name               = var.key_pair_name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.hana.id]
  iam_instance_profile   = aws_iam_instance_profile.hana_s3.name

  root_block_device {
    volume_type = "gp3"
    volume_size = 100
  }

  tags = { Name = "hana-server" }
}

variables.tf

variable "aws_region" {
  default = "us-east-2"
}

variable "key_pair_name" {
  default = "<your-key-pair-name>"
}

variable "hana_media_bucket" {
  default = "<your-s3-bucket-name>"
}

outputs.tf

output "public_ip" {
  value = aws_instance.hana.public_ip
}

output "ssh_command" {
  value = "ssh -i ${var.key_pair_name}.pem ec2-user@${aws_instance.hana.public_ip}"
}

providers.tf

terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
}

hana_install.cfg.j2

[Server]
action=install
components=server
sid={{ hana_sid }}
number={{ hana_instance }}
password={{ hana_master_pw }}
sapadm_password={{ sapadm_password }}
system_user_password={{ hana_master_pw }}
lss_user_password={{ lss_password }}
lss_backup_password={{ lss_password }}
system_usage=custom
install_execution_mode=optimized
sapmnt={{ hana_install_dir }}
datapath={{ hana_data_dir }}/{{ hana_sid }}
logpath={{ hana_log_dir }}/{{ hana_sid }}
ignore=check_min_mem

entry.title: Re: Release 2605 - FA0016 - cannot see inventory
entry.content:
Hi @Ruudvandeven,

Your screenshots are not loading, but if you can see inventories in Manage Floor Stock app, perhaps your data field setup is missing, check Loading Components into the Resource Bins, for:

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel
